flashcards.front: What does IHL stand for in IPv4 header?
flashcards.back: Internet Header Length - indicates the length of the IPv4 header in 32-bit words. Minimum value is 5 (20 bytes), maximum is 15 (60 bytes).

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Identification field in IPv4?
flashcards.back: A 16-bit field used to uniquely identify each fragment of a datagram. All fragments of the same original datagram carry the same Identification value.

flashcards.front: What are the three flags in IPv4 header?
flashcards.back: Reserved (must be 0), Don't Fragment (DF), More Fragments (MF). DF=1 means don't fragment, MF=1 means more fragments follow.

flashcards.front: What is Fragment Offset field used for?
flashcards.back: Indicates the position of the fragment's data relative to the original unfragmented datagram. Measured in 8-byte blocks (except for the last fragment).

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of TTL (Time To Live)?
flashcards.back: Prevents datagrams from circulating indefinitely in the network. Each router decrements TTL by 1; when TTL reaches 0, datagram is discarded.

flashcards.front: What does Protocol field indicate in IPv4?
flashcards.back: Identifies the protocol of the transport layer. Common values: 6=TCP, 17=UDP, 1=ICMP. Used for demultiplexing at the destination.

flashcards.front: How is Header Checksum calculated in IPv4?
flashcards.back: 16-bit one's complement sum of all 16-bit words in the header. Recalculated at each router because TTL changes. Used for error detection.

flashcards.front: What is MTU in network communication?
flashcards.back: Maximum Transmission Unit - the largest packet size that can be transmitted over a network link without fragmentation. Typical Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes.
Fragmentation occurs when a datagram is larger than the MTU of the next network link it needs to traverse. It breaks the datagram into smaller fragments.
Fragmentation is performed by routers at the network layer when the outgoing interface MTU is smaller than the incoming datagram size.
The process of combining fragmented IP datagrams back into the original datagram. It is performed only at the final destination, not at intermediate routers.

flashcards.front: What is the minimum IPv4 header size?
flashcards.back: 20 bytes (without options). This corresponds to IHL value of 5, as each unit in IHL represents 32 bits (4 bytes).

flashcards.front: What happens if the Don't Fragment (DF) flag is set?
flashcards.back: If DF=1, the router cannot fragment the datagram. If the datagram is too large for the MTU, it is discarded and an ICMP Destination Unreachable (Fragmentation Needed) message is sent back.

flashcards.front: What is the size of the IPv4 address?
flashcards.back: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits (4 bytes) long, typically represented as four decimal numbers (0-255) separated by dots (dotted decimal notation).
